CITY OF NE\VPORT BEACH 
August 1, 1980 
OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY 
(714) 640-2201 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
Post Office Box 807 
Sacramento, California 95804 
Attention: 
Dwight Dickerson 
Gentlemen: 
The City of Newport Beach is requesting an opinion as to 
whether or not two members of the Newport Beach Planning 
Commission are permitted to participate in the public hear-
ing process leading to the adoption of the Local Coastal 
Program (LCP) of the City of Newport Beach. 
The City of Newport Beach is presently in the process of 
adopting its LCP as mandated by the Coastal Act of 1976. 
The City has prepared a draft LCP, Land Use Plan and Develop-
ment Policies, which will be the subject of at least two pub-
lic hearings to be conducted by the Newport Beach Planning 
Commission, the first hearing to be held on August 7, 1980. 
It is anticipated that the Planning Commission will take 
"straw votes" with respect to many of the Land Use and Devel-
opment Policies proposed by the draft LCP. 
These proposed 
Policies would be revised or deleted in accordance with these 
votes, the Commission would then take a vote as to the LCP as 
revised, and refer the approved draft to the City Council. 
The City Council will thereafter hold additional public hear-
ings, after which the Council may further revise, and then 
adopt, the proposed LCP. 
The LCP embraces all areas within the City within the Coastal 
Zone. 
This includes approximately 38% of the land area of 
the City of Newport Beach and all the ocean and bay area loca-
ted within the jurisdiction of the City of Newport Beach. 
Within the land area there are approximately 17,000 residential 
units which approximates 55% of the total residential units 
within the City. 
City Hall «I 3300 Newport Boulevard, Newport Beach, California 92663 


Fair Political Practices Commission 
August 1, 1980 
Page Two 
Two members of the Newport Beach Planning Commission own 
property interests within the Coastal Zone subject to the 
LCP. 
We are requesting your opinion as to whether or not 
a conflict of interest exists, as defined in Sections 87100 
and 87103 of the Government Code, such that one or both mem-
bers of the Planning Commission would be precluded from par-
ticipating in the public hearings at the Planning Commission 
relating to the adoption of the LCP. 
Further, we are speci-
fically interested in obtaining an opinion as to the ability 
of these members to participate in the public hearings but 
to abstain from participating in the deliberations and 
decision-making process of the Commission. 
Finally, we fur-
ther desire an opinion as to wether or not the Commissioners, 
should they be deemed to have a financial interest in proper-
ty subject to the LCP, could participate in the deliberations 
and decision-making process of the Planning Commission on 
those portions of the LCP in which they have no financial 
interest. 
As a part of this final opinion request, it should 
be pointed out that the LCP will be approved by the Planning 
Commission with a single vote after its varied parts are con-
sidered and reviewed. 
To enable an opinion to be rendered as quickly as possible, 
we felt it important to include all Land Use Plan and Develop-
ment Policies of the LCP that could relate to the interests of 
the Planning Commissioners involved (see Exhibits A through C) . 
We will also discuss more thoroughly the business and property 
interests of the Planning Commissioners. 
The draft LCP contains 56 pages of background material, a Land 
Use Plan, and Development Policies. 
Many of the policies are 
substantially the same as policies mandated by the Coastal Act 
of 1976. 
Other policies appear to be consistent with the 
Coastal Act policies, but have been tailored to deal with situ-
ations unique to Newport Beach. 
The Land Use Plan consists of text describing certain designated 
geographic areas within the City, which areas have been histori-
cally identified as distinct, and generally commercial, areas 
within the City of Newport Beach. 
In addition to the text, 67 
pages of maps are included specifically identifying land uses 


Fair Political Practices Commission 
August 1, 1980 
Page Three 
within the Coastal Zone. 
The majority of the land area within 
the Coastal Zone is presently designated in the Newport Beach 
General Plan as residential, either single-family or multi-
family. 
There is no proposal in the Land Use Plan which 
would change the residential land uses presently authorized 
by the General Plan and zoning of the City of Newport Beach. 
In the specific area descriptions where a business or property 
interest of one of the Commissioners is located, we are inclu-
ding the text of the area description as well as a copy of the 
maps delineating the LCP Land Use Plan and a larger map loca-
ting the interest within the City of Newport Beach (Exhibit D) . 
We are listing below the specific property and business inter-
ests, each of which exceeds $1,000 in value, of the Planning 
Commissioners involved to assist in the preparation of your 
opinion. 
The facts we believe pertinent are as follows: 
A. 
Commissioner Allen Beek 
1. 
Commissioner Beek owns a one-third interest in 
the Balboa Island ferry. 
The Balboa ferry operates between 
Balboa Island and the Balboa Peninsula (Central Balboa). 
The 
ferry has been in operation, in substantially the same fashion, 
since 1919. 
The majority of the property utilized by the ferry 
and support facilities is situated on private property, owned 
by Mr. Beek's mother, which the Balboa ferry rents from her. 
The ferry landings and a marine railway for ferry maintenance 
are located on tidelands, held in trust by the City of Newport 
Beach, and leased to the Balboa ferry. 
There is no similar 
operation within the City of Newport Beach and, to our knowledge, 
no similar operation has ever been proposed. 
The ferry is a 
coastally dependent use and the Coastal Act, and the LCP, encour-
age, if not require, its continued operation. 
Specific policies 
under "Coastal Dependent Uses" and "Public Property Leaseholds" 
would be applicable to this operation. 
Notwithstanding their 
applicability, given the continuing financial viability of this 
operation, the fact that the ferry is something of an institu-
tion, and the fact that demand for the services provided will 
not be likely to be impacted, this office has difficulty in 
discerning any reasonably foreseeable impact on this operation 
given the adoption of any LCP. 


Fair Political Practices Co~mission 
August 1, 1980 
Page Four 
2. 
Commissioner Beek holds a one-third interest in 
a master lease that covers a large segment of waterfront 
property that includes the Balboa Yacht Basin and Beacon Bay. 
The Balboa Yacht Basin consists of a marina with approximately 
166 slips, a few small co~mercial facilities, and some multi-
family residential structures. 
Beacon Bay consists of 72 
single-family residences. 
These developments are partially 
situated on tidelands held in trust by the City of Newport 
Beach. 
In 1927 the City leased this property to J. A. Beek, 
predecessor to Commissioner Beek. 
The Balboa Yacht Basin was 
subsequently subleased to The Irvine Company, the present day 
operator. 
Beacon Bay was, and is, subleased to the individual 
homeowners. 
All leases expire December 31, 1987. 
The City is 
presently investigating the possibility of releasing some or 
all of this property to the present sublessees under terms and 
conditions that would reflect the current market value of the 
property. 
It is clear that the City is not going to extent or 
renew the master lease to Beek interests. 
The policies set 
forth under "Coastal Dependent Uses" and "Public Property 
Leaseholds" are applicable to this property. 
B. 
Commissioner Paul Balalis 
Commissioner Balalis owns certain parcels of developed 
property in predominantly residential sections within the Newport 
Beach Coastal Zone. 
These holdings will be discussed below. 
Mr. 
Balalis is a licensed real estate broker but does not actively 
participate in the real estate profession within the City of 
Newport Beach. 
Mr. Balalis is a principal in two real estate 
development companies headquartered in the City of Newport Beach 
and leases office space in Lido Village. 
These companies have, 
from time to time, developed real property within the City of 
Newport Beach. 
Commissioner Balalis owns stock in two financial 
institutions having offices within the Coastal Zone in Newport 
Beach. 
One of the institutions, the Bank of Newport, has offices 
in Cannery Village and two other offices on the fringe of the 
Coastal Zone. 
The other institution, Newport Harbour National 
Bank, has a single office located outside, but adjacent to, the 
Coastal Zone. 
Mr. Balalis is a member of the Board of Directors 
of Newport Harbour National Bank. 
It is assumed that both banks 
have a financial interest in property located within the Coastal 
Zone in Newport Beach. 
The draft LCP does not appear to contain 
policies that directly affect either of the banks. 


Fair Political Practices Commission 
August 1, 1980 
Page Five 
1. 
Commissioner Balalis owns three residential 
buildings in the Balboa Peninsula Point area. 
These properties 
are located between C and E streets and front on Balboa Boule-
vard. 
The existing public walkway, the extension of which is 
proposed by the draft LCP, runs along the oceanfront side of 
the buildings. 
The residential character of this area has 
remained relatively unchanged for many years. 
The draft LCP 
land use recommendations are in conformity with the present 
makeup of the area. 
The properties are located in area D, as 
shown on the map which is attached as Exhibit "E." 
As of 
January 1, 1979, there were approximately 1,146 housing units 
within this area. 
2. 
Mr. Balalis owns three residential properties 
situated in an area of the city known as Old Corona del Mar. 
These properties are within the Coastal Zone. 
These properties, 
one of which is presently under construction, are situated within 
areas F2 and F3 shown on Exhibit "E." 
There are approximately 
1,806 housing units within these areas. 
There are no specific 
policies within the draft LCP which directly impact these prop-
erties, nor are there any land use recommendations that reference 
these areas. 
The residential character of these areas has been 
maintained, without significant change, for many years. 
3. 
Commissioner Balalis owns three residential proper-
ties in the West Newport area. 
These properties are situated 
within areas Bl and B2 as shown on Exhibit "E." 
As of January 1, 
1979, there were approximately 1,397 residential units within 
these areas. 
The draft LCP recommends no change in the land use 
designations for the residential areas of West Newport. 
Two of 
the properties are outside the area served by the present walkway, 
but it is unlikely that the extension of the walkway would have 
any bearing on the value of the property. 
The recommended land 
use designations for the exis,ting retail-commercial properties 
that border Pacific Coast Highway in West Newport are in confor-
mance with the existing uses. 
4. 
Commissioner Balalis has an interest in property 
located at 2005 West Balboa Boulevard. 
This property is presently 
in escrow and escrow is scheduled to close August 15, 1980. 
This 
property is located within area B5 on Exhibit "E" and is within 


Fair Political Practices Commission 
August 1, 1980 
Page Six 
the area designated by the LCP as McFadden Square. 
The draft 
LCP land use recommendation is Recreational/Marine Commercial. 
The purpose of such a designation is, according to the LCP, 
"to maintain the existing orientation while allowing an up-
grading of the area." 
Office uses within such an area would 
be limited to space above the first floor and only in cases 
where the ground level is occupied by a permitted use. 
This 
is an area that is presently undergoing a certain amount of 
change, basically in the form of rehabilitation of existing 
structures. 
5. 
Mr. Balalis presently has an interest in property 
located at 227 -
20th Street, Newport Beach. 
This property 
is within McFadden Square Bayfront, as designed by the draft 
LCP, and within area B5 as shown on Exhibit "E." 
The draft 
LCP land use recommendation for this area is Recreational and 
Marine Commercial and Retail and Service Commercial. 
This 
recommended use is consistent with existing uses. 
This prop-
erty is presently being utilized as a boat paint yard, a use 
which would fall within the description of "Coastal Dependent Uses." 
The business is owned and operated by joint property owners. 
This office has reviewed opinions rendered by the FPPC in 
matters bearing some resemblance to the facts of this case. 
In the Matter of Opinion Requested by Ogelsby, 1 FPPC 71; 
In the Matter of Opinion Requested by Biondo, 1 FPPC 77; 
In the Matter of Opinion Requested by Owen, 2 FPPC 77; 
In the Matter of Opinion Requested by Hopkins, 3 FPPC 107; and 
In the Matter of Opinion Requested by Brown, 4 FPPC 19. 
Our 
review of the draft LCP and the property interests discussed 
above leads us to the conclusion that, at least with respect to 
the residential property interests, there is no reasonably fore-
seeable material financial effect, distinguishable from the public 
generally, that would flow from the adoption of the LCP. 
The only 
effect that we could contrive would be the continuing desirability 
of these residential areas through a program that preserves those 
uses that gave the City its charm and ambience. 
This benefit 
would accrue to all residential property owners and fall within 
the holdings of Owen, supra. 


Fair Political Practices Commission 
August 1, 1980 
Page Seven 
While the question is somewhat closer, our opinion is the 
same where commercial properties are concerned. 
Although 
certain policies are directly applicable to these holdings, 
those policies do not require nor recommend, except in very 
limited instances, affirmative conduct on the part of the 
City that would change the status quo. 
The policies that 
relate to coastally dependent uses and visitor serving facili-
ties are general and often contradict one another (e.g., en-
courage such uses but limit the ability to construct them in 
light of environmental and public access concerns) such that 
their potential effect is difficult or impossible to predict. 
The possibility that adoption of the LCP would tend to pre-
serve existing marine uses, thus restricting competition, is 
offset by the fact that owners of property that house such 
uses are restricted from developing their property in a more 
profitable manner. 
The analysis of the impact of the LCP on 
commercial properties is similar to that undertaken in 
inquiry "C" in Owen, supra, and the result should be similar. 
We realize that you will receive this material shortly before 
the first scheduled Planning Commission public hearing, and we 
ask that you advise us by phone, if possible as to your 
thoughts with respect to the participation of the two commis-
sioners in the hearings thereafter abstaining from delibera-
tions and/or voting on issues which may relate to their holdings.

sections.question: The City of Newport Beach is requesting an opinion as to whether or not two members of the Newport Beach Planning Commission are permitted to participate in the public hearing process leading to the adoption of the Local Coastal Program (LCP) of the City of Newport Beach. Specifically: (1) whether a conflict of interest exists under Sections 87100 and 87103 of the Government Code such that one or both members would be precluded from participating in the public hearings; (2) whether these members can participate in public hearings but abstain from deliberations and decision-making; and (3) whether Commissioners with financial interests in property subject to the LCP could participate in deliberations on portions of the LCP in which they have no financial interest, given that the LCP will be approved by a single vote.
sections.question_synthetic: Do two Planning Commission members with property interests in the Coastal Zone subject to the proposed Local Coastal Program have a disqualifying conflict of interest under Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103, and if so, what forms of limited participation are permitted?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from City of Newport Beach to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No conclusion section present. Document contains detailed factual background about two commissioners (Allen Beek and Paul Balalis) and their respective property/business interests. Text is partially corrupted at beginning but main request letter is legible. Document appears to be page 1-6 of a longer submission with exhibits.

embedding.summary: The City of Newport Beach requests FPPC advice on whether two Planning Commission members with property interests in the Coastal Zone can participate in public hearings and deliberations regarding adoption of the Local Coastal Program, citing specific property holdings and business interests of each commissioner.
